coredata.dc:description: An extensive study into novel materials and compositional changes has resulted in the invention of high-performance supercapattery electrodes. This research focuses on the hydrothermal synthesis of magnesium manganese sulfide, followed by the integration of graphene quantum dots (MgMnS@GQDs) to assess their electrochemical capabilities. By employing super-concentrated aqueous electrolytes, often referred to as “water in salt” (WIS) solutions, it is possible to achieve a significantly reduced water activity, thereby expanding the electrochemical stability window (ESW). This work presents a new hybrid electrolyte that alternatively combines water with sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) and acetonitrile (AN). At a current of 2.0 A/g, the MgMnS@GQDs electrode demonstrated a maximum capacity of 812 C/g (325 F/g). The MgMnS@GQDs//AC achieved 104 C/g capacity (35 F/g), 49 Wh/kg energy density, and 1150 W/kg energy density in two electrode configurations. It showed an overpotential of 125 mV and a 47 mV/dec Tafel slope in the HER test.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An extensive study into novel materials and compositional changes has resulted in the invention of high-performance supercapattery electrodes. This research focuses on the hydrothermal synthesis of magnesium manganese sulfide, followed by the integration of graphene quantum dots (MgMnS@GQDs) to assess their electrochemical capabilities. By employing super-concentrated aqueous electrolytes, often referred to as “water in salt” (WIS) solutions, it is possible to achieve a significantly reduced water activity, thereby expanding the electrochemical stability window (ESW). This work presents a new hybrid electrolyte that alternatively combines water with sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) and acetonitrile (AN). At a current of 2.0 A/g, the MgMnS@GQDs electrode demonstrated a maximum capacity of 812 C/g (325 F/g). The MgMnS@GQDs//AC achieved 104 C/g capacity (35 F/g), 49 Wh/kg energy density, and 1150 W/kg energy density in two electrode configurations. It showed an overpotential of 125 mV and a 47 mV/dec Tafel slope in the HER test.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of their higher power densities and extended cycle life, supercapacitors (SC) have lately attracted considerable interest in energy storage systems (ESS) [1,2]. The hybrid devices bridge the gap between regular capacitors and batteries by offering a unique combination of substantial power density and improved cycle stability [3].

In the field of energy storage, the choice of electrode and electrolyte is critical [4]. Because of inherent limitations, the working potential of water-based electrolytes is frequently restricted to less than 1.2 V [5]. Organic electrolytes, on the other hand, have the advantage of being able to work at higher voltages, reaching 2.8 V [6]. They provide greater work efficiency and stronger electrochemical stability. The disadvantages of each type can be addressed independently by hybrid systems that mix electrolytes. It widens the window, has strong storage capacity and improves electrochemical performance [7].

The choice of electrode material directly affects charge and discharge, capacity and energy density, making it an important factor in the operation of the supercapacitor. An important way to improve the performance of supercapacitors and enable many applications in energy storage and other fields is by tuning the electrode material. Due to their unique and good electrical properties, metal sulfides (MS) have become excellent choices as SC electrode materials and HER electrocatalysts [8]. These sulfides have diverse adjustable compositions and morphologies, enabling customised electrode design that meets individual energy storage needs. MSs with high conductivity and an abundance of redox-active sites, such as cobalt sulfide and nickel sulfide, provide efficient charge transfer and improved capacitance [9]. Bimetallic sulfides, generated by mixing two distinct metal sulfides (nickel cobalt sulfide and manganese iron sulfide), display synergistic effects that improve their electrochemical properties even more [10]. Furthermore, these materials frequently exhibit pseudocapacitive responses that allow faradaic charge storage techniques in addition to double-layer capacitance, boosting total energy storage capacities [11].

Magnesium and manganese sulfide have received a lot of interest as prospective materials for electrodes, each with its own set of advantages. Guolang Zhou et al. [12] reported CoNiMg heterostructure for supercapacitor applications, which deliver 333 C/g capacity in a three-electrode design. Manganese sulfide (MnS) is a well-known pseudocapacitive material with outstanding electronic conductivity in several research. MnS has an electrical conductance of roughly 3.2 × 1
0 3
S / cm , this is notable when compared to similar oxides or hydroxides [13,14]. Excellent electrical conductivity of the material is a crucial quality for efficient charge transfer, which improves the electrochemical efficiency. Additionally, MnS displays a wide range of shapes and is simple to synthesise, offering a variety of electrode designs [15]. In a current investigation by Tang et al. [14], MnS nanostructures were shown to offer enormous promise as electrodes for applications in SCs. The MnS produced a high capacitance of 704 F/g at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. By combining the high specific capacitance of the binary metal sulfides with the better electrical conductivity and stability offered by the dopant, doping capacitive graded materials into binary metal sulfides for supercapacitors results in improved performance. The result is a higher power and energy density, making Supercapacitors more efficient and durable. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a special type of quantum dots, usually a few nanometers in diameter. They blend the best features of graphene and classic quantum dots, such as large specific area, high efficiency, and adjustable band gap.

MgMnS@GQDs electrodes are produced using the hydrothermal technique. The electrochemical performance of MgMnS@GQDs was tested in three distinct aqueous-organic (WIS/AN) electrolytes. Electrochemical characterisation of MgMnS@GQDs was done using three-cell and two-cell designs. MgMnS@GQDs were also tested in asymmetric supercapacitors and HER reactions.

The Merc was selected to deliver activated carbon, acetonitrile (AN), and graphene quantum dots. GQDs have a conductivity of 102 S/m and an average size of 4 nm. Furthermore, GQDs should have a surface area per gram of 423 m2/g. Thermo Fisher Scientific supplied N-methyl 2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and carbon black. Sigma Aldrich supplied manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)2, magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2, N-polyvinylidene-fluoride (PVDF), nickel foam, sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S.9H2O), platinum wire, and Ag/AgCl.

Hydrothermal synthesis is selected to synthesise MgMnS@GQDs in a closed system under high pressure and extreme temperatures (Fig. 1 ). We synthesise four materials (MgS, MnS, MgMnS, and MgMnS@GQDs). In the main manuscript, we discussed only the best electrode material (MgMnS@GQDs), while the other materials were presented in the supplementary section. To synthesise MgS, a 10 mmol Mg(NO3)2 solution of Mg(NO3)2 was made into 60 mL of DIW. The resultant solution was transported to a high-pressure autoclave, sealed, and heated to 180o C for 6–8 h with a clean piece of nickel foam (NF). Following a 6 h incubation at 120 °C, the precursors that had formed on the Ni foam were obtained. Subsequently, the Ni foam and the precursors were immersed in a 0.16 M sodium sulfide solution and held at 160 °C for an additional 6 h. The resulting samples were retrieved after thorough washing with deionised water and ethanol. The autoclave was permitted to cool normally after hydrothermal heating, and the resultant precipitate was recognised as MgS.

S → MgS + 2 NaN
O 3

Similarly, 10 mmol of Mn(NO3)2 was added to 60 mL of DIW to synthesise MnS. Following that, the preceding processes were repeated to synthesise MnS.
(2)
Mn
N

a 2
S → MnS + 2 NaN
O 3

To synthesise MgMnS, 10 mmol of Mn(NO3)2, 10 mmol of Mg(NO3)2 and 12 mmol of urea were dissolved in 60 mL of DIW. Subsequently, the solution thus acquired was introduced into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, with a pristine Ni foam element included. Following a 6 h incubation period at 120 °C, the resulting precursors had successfully developed on the surface of the Ni foam. Following that, the Ni foam containing these precursors was submerged in a 0.16 M sodium sulfide mixture and kept at 160 °C for an additional 6 h.

A liquid solution was produced for the production of MgMnS@GQDs nanocomposites by combining Mg(NO3)2 (8 mmol), Mn(NO3)2 (8 mmol), urea (12 mmol), and 10 mg of GQDs in 60 mL of deionised water. The resulting solution was then put into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with a clean Ni foam substrate and followed the abovementioned strategy.

Three different molar ratios of acetonitrile, salt, and water were used to prepare the electrolytic solution. We study three specific examples: 1:1:1 (33:33:33 %), called WIS/AN-1; 2:1:1 (50:25:25 %), WIS/AN-2 control; and 3:1.5:1.5 (60:20:20) reported as WIS/AN-3. Sodium perchlorate breaks down during electrochemical processes resulting in sodium ions (Na+) and perchlorate ions (ClO4 −). These ions then interact with solvents, resulting in solvated entities. During electrolysis, oxidative activities occur at the anode. Sodium ions are reduced at the cathode. The electrolyte ratio must be properly blended and measured to get the correct composition. The electrolyte solution that resulted was utilised to assess the electrochemical properties.

Rigaku Ultima III X-ray diffractometer was used to assess crystal structure and phase uniformity. A Hitachi-S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to study the material's surface. Elemental analysis were identified using Thermo Pittsburgh-XPS technology. The optical properties of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS@GQDs were evaluated using a UV–Vis (Thermo Fisher-spectrophotometer). Raman spectroscopy (Bruker Senterra Raman spectrometer) was used to verify the chemical states. It was possible to monitor critical electrochemical parameters using the Corrtest CS300 workstation.

The electrochemical properties of the MgMnS@GQDs materials in three different electrolytes was evaluated using three distinct techniques. Since the material was directly grown on nickel foam, there is no need for any sort of binder that reduces the performance. By weighing the NF before and after the material growth, it was feasible to calculate the weight of the active material. For the MgMnS@GQDs electrode, the weight of the active material on the NF as a consequence was between 5.7 mg. The working electrode was subsequently heated in an oven at 60 °C to achieve adequate solidification and adherence to the substrate's slurry. The specific capacity for MgMnS@GQDs composite was measured through CV and GCD using the equations below [16].
(3)

I × VdV is the integrated area. While in Eq. (4), I × t represent the multiple of discharge time and current.

A sophisticated hybrid device was successfully built for practical applications, exploiting the exceptional capabilities of MgMnS@GQDs as the anode and the excellent features of activated carbon (AC) as the cathode. Energy and power density were estimated using the formula below [17].
(6)
E =
Q × Δ V
2 × 3.6
(7)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
While E represents energy density, QV is the combination of specific capacity and work capacity in Eq. (6). In formula (7), t is the oscillation time.

Multiple essential factors were rigorously examined throughout the electrochemical testing. A thorough knowledge of the electrochemical performance of the hybrid device was gained by integrating the essential information retrieved from CV, GCD, and stability investigations, opening the way for prospective innovations in energy storage technology.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, depicted in Fig. 2a, was carried out to scrutinise the crystal structure and phase composition of the synthesised MgMnS@GQDs material. The XRD patterns of MgS exhibited distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 35°, 52°, and 60.3°, which perfectly matched with the crystallographic planes (200), (220), and (222), respectively. These results align closely with the reference from the JCPDS: 35-0730, unequivocally indicating a cubic crystal structure for MgS [18]. In parallel, the XRD analysis of MnS displayed notable diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.2°, 39.8°, 50°, and 59.4°, which corresponded to the crystal planes (100), (102), (103), and (311), respectively, as per the JCPDS: 65-3413. These findings strongly correlate with the tetragonal crystal structure of MnS, affirming the successful synthesis of MnS with well-defined crystalline properties [19]. Remarkably, when examining the XRD pattern of MgMnS@GQDs, it exhibited a pattern that exactly mirrored the individual XRD patterns of MgS and MnS, providing unambiguous evidence of the successful formation of MgMnS within the material. Furthermore, the presence of GQDs was corroborated by the presence of the (002) plane at 2θ = 24.8° [20]. Sherre's formula was also applied to calculate the crystallite size.
(8)
D =
Kλ
β cos θ
In Eq. (8), the crystallite size is represented by D , K is the constant, λ is the wavelength, and θ is the diffraction angle. MgMnS@GQDs crystallite size was 68 nm.

SEM was used to investigate the surface morphology and measure the surface appearance and microstructural properties of the binary composite MgMnS@GQD, as shown in Fig. 2(b). SEM examination revealed interesting details about the surface properties and microstructure of MgMnS composites. In surface studies, the presence of nanoscale aggregates and clusters was observed and the composition of the binary composites was confirmed. These aggregates were found to have distinct aggregates, indicating that MgMnS and GQD aggregates are well integrated at the nanoscale level. The uneven distribution of these components in the composite matrix indicates the accuracy and sensitivity of the synthesis process.

Raman spectroscopy was used to analyse the vibrational and physical properties of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS@GQDs (Fig. 2c). A strong peak was observed between 445 and 628 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrational structure due to the MgS crystal configuration. These peaks are produced by bending and stretching vibrations of SMgS bonds. Raman analysis of MnS shows clear peaks in different regions. The main peaks were observed at 227, 372, and 685 cm, corresponding to the vibrational patterns of the MnS crystal structure. These peaks correspond to bending and stretching oscillations of the material SMnS bonds. The Raman spectrum of the MgMnS@GQDs binary composite shows a difference that can be attributed to the MgS and MnS components. These peaks correspond to the Raman spectra of individual MgS and MnS materials and indicate the presence of two components in the mixture. MgMnS@GQDs have separate peaks at roughly 445 and 628 cm−1 (corresponding to MgS) as well as 227, 372, and 616 cm−1 (which corresponds to MnS. Additionally, the Raman peaks related to GQDs were not explicitly mentioned in the provided information. However, it can be inferred that the GQDs' vibrational characteristics, including the D (disorder-induced) at 1375 cm−1 and G (graphitic) bands at 1589 cm−1, were also present in the FTIR spectra. The D band typically arises due to structural defects and disorder in the carbon lattice, while the G band is associated with the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms, indicative of graphitic structures. The specific wavenumbers for these bands would depend on the specific nature and structure of the GQDs used in the composite.

The optical properties of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS@GQDs materials through UV–Vis thin film methodology employing absorption techniques (Fig. 2d). The UV–Vis spectra revealed different absorption characteristics and information on the position of the absorption peaks, indicating the electronic transitions occurring inside the materials.

The large absorption peak found in the UV–Vis spectra of MgS about 210 nm is relevant in terms of devices that store energy. This peak represents the band gap energy, which is important in understanding the material's electrical transitions and possible applicability in such devices [21].

The main absorption peak at about 340 nm in the UV–visible spectrum of MnS indicates its different energy. This strong absorption, especially in the ultraviolet region, indicates the energy required for the electronic transfer of MnS from the valence band to the conduction band. The ability of MnS to capture light in this range allows the optimisation of light absorption and energy conversion processes in these devices [22]. The significant absorption peak at 283 nm further confirmed the presence of GQDs. The location of these peaks in the UV–visible spectrum proves that two elements are present in the compound.

The XPS examination of Mg 2p, Mn 2p, and S 2p is shown in Fig. 3(a–c). The Mg 2p1/2 spike usually has a larger binding energy (BE) than the Mg 2p3/2 peak. The binding energy for the Mg 2p1/2 peak is normally about 45.2 eV, whereas the BE for the Mg 2p3/2 spike typically falls around 51.3 eV (Fig. 3a) [23]. The Mn 2p XPS spectra exhibit several peaks caused by spin-orbit splitting, allowing the detection of distinct Mn oxidation states as well as coordination environments. Mn 2p spectra have two main spikes. Usually, the Mn 2p1/2 spike has a larger BE than the Mn 2p3/2 spike. The BE for the Mn 2p1/2 spike is usually about 640 eV, whereas the Mn 2p3/2 spike is usually around 655 eV (Fig. 3b) [24]. Finally, the S 2p XPS spectrum displays typical peaks associated with the BE of the sulfur atom, which aids in the investigation of its chemical bonding and oxidation states. The S 2p spectra have two notable peaks. The S 2p1/2 spike often has a larger binding energy than the S 2p3/2 peak. The S 2p1/2 peak normally appeared at 161.2 eV, whereas the S 2p3/2 spike at 165 eV (Fig. 3c) [25]. The C 1s peak exhibited three distinct subpeaks at approximately 282.5 eV (CC, sp2 carbon), 285.7 eV (COC, epoxy), and 289.3 eV (OCO, carbonyl), signifying the graphitic structure of GQDs and the presence of oxygen-containing groups (Fig. 3d). The O 1s peak at 534 eV confirmed the existence of CO (carbonyl) functionalities (Fig. 3e). The N 1s peak displayed subpeaks at approximately 397.2 eV (pyridinic N), 400 eV (pyrrolic N), and 402.5 eV (graphitic N), indicating the presence of nitrogen in various functional groups and the graphitic structure of the GQDs.

To determine the surface area of the surface (SA) of the MgMnS and MgMnS@GQDs, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used. The IV-type BET isotherm revealed the mesoporous nature of MgMnS and MgMnS@GQDs [26]. The SSA calculated for individual MgMnS was 124.35 m2/g (Fig. 3g). The largest number of pores in MgS had a size of roughly 0.17 nm, indicating a very narrow distribution of pore sizes based on the pore size distribution analysis. According to the BET study, MgMnS@GQDs have a higher SSA of 133.35 m2/g than MgMnS (Fig. 3h). This suggests a larger surface area available for adsorption and probable interactions. With a 0.017 cm3/g pore volume, the MgMnS@GQDs had a greater pore capability for absorbed molecules. Following the distribution of pore dimensions analysis, MgMnS@GQDs has a wider variety of pore sizes, with an average pore size of roughly 0.21 nm. There are multiple reasons for the higher pore volume, bigger pore size, and improved surface area of the MgMnS@GQDs composite compared to the single MgMnS material. Incorporating Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) into the composite offers a distinct nanostructured architecture in addition to an extra carbonaceous component. GQDs have a large specific surface area since they are two-dimensional, which implies there are many active sites for gas adsorption and a greater surface area for reactions. The total surface area, pore size, and pore volume of all synthesised materials are shown in Table 1 .

Utilising cyclic voltammetry (CV), the electrochemical characteristics of MgMnS@GQDs were studied. Fig. S1 represents the optimised potential window for MgMnS@GQDs in WIS/AN-3 electrolyte. The CV tests were performed using a scan rate of 5–50 mV/s, using a sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) salt with acetonitrile (AN) in three different molar ratios (WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3). The main manuscript discusses the best material MgMnS@GQDs composite in aqueous WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3 electrolytes. The electrochemical evaluation of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS were discussed in the supplementary part. The working potential (WP) in aqueous electrolytes was 0–2.5 eV, which was maintained. The CV curves for MgMnS@GQDs revealed various characteristics of the electrochemical properties. The CV curves, in particular, showed classic oxidation and reduction spikes, indicating battery-like behaviour as shown in Fig. 4(a–c). The oxidation spike reflects the anodic process, in which the material loses electrons and experiences oxidation, whereas the reduction surge indicates the cathodic reaction, in which the material gains electrons and experiences reduction [27]. The form of the CV graphs indicates the electrochemical activity and resilience of the materials. The appearance of distinct oxidation and reduction spikes shows that charge transfer kinetics are efficient and that the redox process is reversible [28]. This battery-like behaviour holds promise for applications requiring great cycle stability and charge storage capabilities. In addition, the CV curves demonstrated outstanding stability even at higher scan rates. The survival of distinct oxidation and reduction peaks in the unaltered form of the curves indicates that substances retain their electrochemical reactions and endurance even when scanned at greater rates. MgMnS@GQDs have better stability due to the structural integrity of the compounds, efficient charge transfer channels, and the absence of significant side reactions. The CV analyses for MgS, MnS, and MgMnS are shown in Fig. S2.

Along with the CV evaluation, galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests were also carried out to elaborate further on the electrochemical properties of MgS, MnS, MgMnS, and MgMnS@GQDs. The GCD tests for MgS, MnS, and MgMnS are represented in Fig. S3. The voltage range and electrolyte solutions employed in these studies were the same as previously described. The charge retention capability of the material was evaluated with current densities ranging from 2.0 to 3.5 A/g. The GCD charts for MgMnS@GQDs showed a prominent hump, which was typical of battery-like activity, as shown in Fig. 4(d–f). The form of the GCD graphs gave vital information about the materials' electrochemical characteristics. MgMnS@GQDs in the WIS/AN-3 electrolyte system, in particular, have a larger discharging plateau zone than the WIS/AN-1 and WIS/AN-2 systems. This prolonged discharge plateau region indicates superior electrochemical activity, implying increased charge storage capability and reversibility of redox processes. The extended length of the discharge plateau found in the final ratio (WIS/AN-3) could be due to its composition. This ratio, which contains 60 % water and 20 % salt and acetonitrile, is believed to promote better ion mobility and conductance within the electrolyte. WIS/AN-3's greater water content may help in ion diffusion, encouraging improved electrolyte wetting of the electrodes and allowing for improved charge transfer mechanisms during discharge. When contrasted with the other ratios (WIS/AN-1 and WIS/AN-2) this optimal balance of water, salt, and acetonitrile ratios likely contributes to lower resistance, allowing for a longer discharge duration. In addition, the GCD curves indicated the great stability of the MgMnS@GQDs in all three molar ratios of WIS/AN electrolyte. Although the differences in the constitution and electrochemical characteristics, the form of the GCD curves remained constant demonstrating uniform and reliable charge/discharge activity. Material stability is critical in electrochemical storage devices because it assures long-term performance and cycle durability.

We used EIS tests to analyse the characteristics of MgMnS@GQDs in three distinct electrolytic solutions: WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3. EIS measurements give useful information on the interfacial behaviour and transfer of charge rates of electrodes, making them an effective tool for characterising their electrochemical characteristics. The EIS spectra of MgMnS@GQDs revealed different characteristics indicating their varied electrochemical behaviour as represented in Fig. 4(g–i). A prominent semicircle was observed in the EIS spectrum of MgMnS@GQDs in all three electrolytes. This was typically linked with battery-like behaviour and offers information on the kinetics and stability of the electrode material [29]. The MgMnS@GQDs showed wider semicircle in WIS/AN-3 electrolyte which suggests possible benefits in electrochemical stability and lower charge transfer resistance, presumably due to the synergistic impact of mixing MgMnS and GQDs. We concentrated on examining the equivalent series resistance (ESR) values derived for each material. ESR is the entire resistance of the system, including electrolyte resistance, electrode/electrolyte interface resistance, and any internal resistance inside the material. Acetonitrile (AN) loses some of its resistivity when NiCl4 is added. Table S1 shows the equivalent series resistance of MgMnS@GQDs in WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3 electrolytes. As seen in Fig. 4(h), the equivalent series resistance (ESR) significantly decreases as NiCl4 concentration increases compared to acetonitrile (AN) by roughly eight times. The EIS spectrum for acetonitrile (AN) is represented in Fig. S4. The results give insight into the total resistance of the materials and can be used to predict their electrochemical performance.

The specific capacity for MgMnS@GQDs in all three electrolytes was measured through CV at various scan rates and through GCD at different current densities as indicated in Fig. 5(a) and (d). CV and GCD methods were used to compare the electrochemical properties of MgMnS@GQDs in all three electrolytes. The specific capacity of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS are shown in Fig. S5. CV observations were taken at a scan rate of 5 mV/s, and GCD studies were carried out at 2.0 A/g. Fig. 5(b) and (e) shows the comparison plots produced for MgMnS@GQDs in three electrolytes (WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3), respectively. The CV curves in Fig. 5(b) provide information about the redox behaviour and charge storage capacities of the materials. The specific capacity values for MgMnS@GQDs have been determined to be 493 C/g (197 F/g) in WIS/AN-1, 623 C/g (249 F/g) in WIS/AN-2, and 790 C/g (316 F/g) in WIS/AN-3 after integrating the area beneath the CV curve (Fig. 5c). This specific capacity (Qs) represents the amount of charge held per unit mass of material and highlights the differences in charge storage capabilities among the various compositions. Fig. 5(e) depicts the charge/discharge activity and energy storage capability of the MgMnS@GQDs using GCD plots. The GCD curves yielded specific capacity values of 527 C/g (210.8 F/g) in WIS/AN-1, 641 C/g (256 F/g) in WIS/AN-2, and 812 C/g (325 F/g) in WIS/AN-3 (Fig. 5f). GCD analysis yielded greater specific capacity values than CV analysis.

When compared with single-component materials, the synergistic effect leads to better electrochemical performance [30]. The addition of MgS to the MnS adds more redox-active sites, increasing the charge storage capacity of MgMnS@GQDs [31]. Furthermore, the inclusion of manganese in the composite can improve the resilience and structural integrity. The EIS chart shows that the improved charge transfer capacity leads to decreased equivalent series resistance (ESR) values [32]. The composite's linked network of MgS and MnS improves ion transport inside the electrode material, facilitating quicker ion diffusion and enhanced electrochemical activity. Numerous variables contribute to the superior electrochemical results of the 3:1.5:1.5 (WIS/AN-3) ratio in CV, GCD, and EIS tests when compared with the other two combinations (WIS/AN-1 and WIS/AN-2). First, compared to the other ratios, WIS/AN-3 has a greater molar water ratio. Water is commonly used as an electrolyte in electrochemical processes and can improve ionic conductivity.

Finally, the lower impedance in the EIS experiments was most likely owing to WIS/AN-3's improved electrochemical performance. The increased water content, balanced salt and acetonitrile ratios, and better ion mobility would result in a lower resistance to charge transfer, implying that WIS/AN-3 is an even better electrolyte solution for electrochemical activities.

A supercapattery was built to boost energy storage capacity by pairing MgMnS@GQDs with activated carbon (AC). Fig. 6(a) depicts a schematic illustration of the supercapattery. The reversible redox processes associated with the MgMnS@GQDs composite cause this faradaic activity. The supercapattery functioned at a combined operational potential of 0–3 V, spanning the AC and MgMnS@GQDs operating voltage ranges. The supercapattery system developed an extended voltage window by using the complementing electrochemical characteristics of the two materials, allowing for more energy storage capacity. The rectangular form of the CV curve at lower potential suggests its outstanding capacitive behaviour, with high power capabilities and quick charge/discharge rates. The faradaic form of the CV curve for MgMnS@GQDs at higher potential shows that it may store charge via reversible redox processes, resulting in greater energy storage capacity [33]. The integration of these two distinct characteristics enables it to bring together the advantages of both EDLC and batteries, leading to high energy and power densities. A CV analysis was performed to test the electrochemical stability and charge storage capabilities of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery (Fig. 6b).

GCD analysis was performed on the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapacitor to measure charge storage and energy transfer at different currents (Fig. 6c). The curve shows the characteristic plateau during unloading, indicating the existence of stable storage. The large plateau area observed in the GCD curve indicates the improved electrochemical stability of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapacitor because it allows power to be transferred without loss for a longer period of time.

The determined b-value from the CV graph falls within the normal supercapattery range of 0.5 to 0.8 (Fig. 6d). It demonstrates that the supercapattery can successfully store and release charge via both faradic and adsorption/desorption mechanisms. At an unchanged current density of 1.0 A/g, the capacity of MgMnS@GQDs//AC was evaluated using CV at 10 mV/s (Fig. 6e) and GCD at 1.0 A/g (Fig. 6f). The capacity was calculated to be 97 C/g (32 F/g) by CV and 104 C/g (35 F/g) by GCD. The high specific capacity of MgMnS@GQDs//AC was104 C/g, which displays its better charge-storing capabilities. GQDs in MgMnS composite boost chemical processes and reverse charge preservation, while activated carbon improves double-layer capacitance, resulting in a higher overall specific capacity. The MgMnS@GQDs//AC system's combination of redox reactions and double-layer capacitance provides an even and efficient charge storage mechanism, resulting in higher specific capacity and better electrochemical efficiency. The Qs of 104 C/g obtained from GCD analysis at 1.0 A/g verifies the suitability.

The endurance of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC was evaluated using a demanding test consisting of 8000 GCD repeats, as shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b). The purpose of this test was to determine the long-term efficacy and capabilities of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC device to maintain its electrochemical properties over time. The stability test findings validated the device's exceptional lifetime and reliability. After 8000 GCD cycles, the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery displayed exceptional stability, with a columbic efficiency of 93 %. MgMnS@GQDs//AC device demonstrated a high columbic performance of 93 %, showing that its charge retention and release abilities were effectively maintained even after extended cycles. In addition, the retained capacity of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC was 85 % after 8000 GCD cycles. The MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery has good capacity retention of 85 %, indicating exceptional stability and low capacity degradation during extensive cycling. Table 2 represents the comparison of columbic efficiency and capacity retention of MgMnS@GQDs//AC device with literature. The EIS analysis before and after 8000 cycles was also performed (Fig. 7c). It can be observed from the EIS spectrum that the ESR value rises to 0.89 U from 0.75 U after 8000 cycles.

The amount of energy stored per unit mass is referred to as energy density
E d
, whereas power density
P d
denotes the speed at which the stored energy can be supplied. The MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery had a high Ed of 49 Wh/kg and a high P
d of 1150 W/kg (Fig. 7d). The achieved energy density of 49 Wh/kg indicates the potential of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC device to store a significant quantity of energy, making it appropriate for applications requiring sustained energy delivery. The substantial power density of 1150 W/kg illustrates its ability to transport energy swiftly, resulting in efficient power output. Table 3 compares the Ed and P
d of MgMnS@GQDs to the literature.

The application of Dunn's approach in the hybrid electrolyte (WIS/AN-3) at various scan rates provided valuable insights into the capacitive and diffusive participation (Fig. 8 ).

The findings demonstrated a significant variation in the influence of capacitive and diffusive mechanisms. The capacitive involvement was discovered to be 19.3 % at 10 mV/s. This implies that the faradic behaviour of the supercapattery accounts for a large amount of charge storage during electrochemical operations. At 10 mV/s, redox reactions represent the supremacy of the storage mechanism. This behaviour is related to the reversible incorporation of ions into the lattice structure of the electrode material, which enables the storage of higher quantities of charge. In most cases, this process results in a slower diffusion of ions and a larger specific capacity, but at the expense of a lower power density. Meanwhile, at 100 mV/s, the capacitive involvement increased significantly to 35.93 %. At 100 mV/s, the capacitive behaviour appears to play a larger role in charge storage as compared to the low scan rate. The electrical double-layer development and the quick ion adsorption and desorption at the electrode-electrolyte interface bring on this behaviour. The capacitive charge storage is primarily surface-driven, providing better power densities but lower energy densities. The quicker kinetics associated with the capacitive charge storage mechanism might explain increased capacitive involvement at higher scans. The device showed 27.75 % capacitive participation at 50 mV/s. Diffusive participation is linked to redox reactions or faradaic processes, in which charge storage happens via reversible chemical reactions inside the electrode materials.

We examined the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) electrocatalytic activity of MgS, MnS, and MgMnS@GQDs. The tests were performed in a three-electrode arrangement with a scan rate of 2 mV/s in a WIS/AN-3 electrolyte, and the catalyst loading was adjusted to 2.2 mg/cm2. We also examined commercial Pt/C coated on NF with a Pt loading of 2.2 mg/cm2 for the sake of comparison. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves obtained with a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) are shown in Fig. 9(a). Notably, the HER performance of the MgMnS@GQDs electrode was quite effective. With the lowest onset potential and the least overpotential, the MgMnS@GQDs electrode demonstrated HER activity equivalent to that of commercial Pt/C.

The HER efficiency of the MgMnS@GQDs/NF electrode is excellent and almost equivalent to that of Pt/C used in industry. It has the lowest onset potential, which is extremely near to Pt/C, and can be driven by a Js of 10 mA/cm2 with a very small overpotential of 60 mV. This overpotential is just 8 mV greater than that of Pt catalyst (52 mV), and it surpasses other metal sulfide-based catalysts including MgS/NF (121 mV) and hollow MnS nanoparticles (107 mV).

In alkaline circumstances, it even competes favourably with other HER catalysts made of non-noble metals. Pt/C exhibits a Tafel slope of 43 mV/dec, according to the Tafel plots in Fig. 9(b), which shows that a Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism is responsible for the HER. MgMnS@GQDs/NF showed a Tafel slope of 47 mV/dec outperforming the MgS/NF (67 mV/dec) and MnS/NF (61 mV/dec). The comparison of the necessary overpotentials at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 and Tafel slope for all samples were represented in Fig. 9(c). MgMnS@GQDs has higher HER activity, evidenced by its lowest overpotential and shortest Tafel slope. Table 4 was also made for comparison.

When assessing catalysts, stability is a key issue to take into account. This investigation used cyclic voltammetry scans in the range of 0.20 to −0.20 V versus RHE at a fast scan rate of 100 mV/s to check the durability of the MgMnS@GQDs/NF. The polarisation curve nearly exactly mirrored the first one after the electrode underwent 5000 cycles in 1 M KOH, suggesting exceptional stability (Fig. 9d). Using bulk electrolysis of water, the long-term electrochemical reliability of the electrode was also assessed. Fig. 9(e) shows a minor drop over 200 h in the overpotentials. The outstanding performance of MgMnS@GQDs composite in energy storage and hydrogen evolution reaction makes it an appealing choice for future energy requirements.

In this work, the MgMnS@GQDs electrode was tested in three distinct molar ratios of water to NiCl4 to acetonitrile (WIS/AN-1, WIS/AN-2, and WIS/AN-3). The MgMnS@GQDs composite had greater specific capacities in WIS/AN-3 (3:1.5:1.5) as compared to WIS/AN-1 (1:1:1) and WIS/AN-2 (2:1:1). The MgMnS@GQDs electrode showed high specific capacity of 812 C/g (325 F/g) in WIS/AN-3 electrolytic solution. Furthermore, the resilience of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery was demonstrated by the preserved columbic efficiency and capacity retention of 93 % and 85 %, respectively, after 8000 GCD cycles. The energy density of the MgMnS@GQDs//AC supercapattery was 49 Wh/kg, along with 1150 W/kg power density thus demonstrating its applicability for both high energy storage capacity and quick power delivery. The HER test indicated a Tafel slope of 47 mV/dec for MgMnS@GQDs.
